items.response: Before you sign: find the move-out notice period (usually 30–60 days), the pet policy, and what counts as normal wear and tear. Everything you don't read now, you agree to forever. Take twenty minutes. It's a legally binding document.
items.intent: Encourage new renters to actually read and understand the key terms of a lease.

items.prompt: Generate a NPS survey question for a fast food drive-through
items.response: How likely are you to recommend our drive-through to someone who sets realistic expectations before pulling up to the speaker?
items.intent: Measure customer satisfaction with speed and accuracy of a drive-through order.

items.prompt: Create a voicemail transcript from an automated package delivery notification that failed to deliver
items.response: Hello, this is an automated delivery notice from Parcel Direct. We attempted to deliver your package today at 1:47 PM but were unable to complete the delivery. A second attempt will be made tomorrow. Alternatively, you may pick up your parcel at our facility on Commerce Drive using confirmation number 8834-Q. Thank you for choosing Parcel Direct.
items.intent: Simulate the mild despair of a missed-delivery robocall.

items.prompt: Draft humorous wedding vows from a person to their password manager
items.response: I vow never to use your autofill and then wonder why the field is empty. I promise to set a master password I will actually remember, and to write it in the one physical location that will survive me. I will add new logins promptly, rather than using 'forgot password' as my primary authentication strategy. You remember what I cannot. You keep me honest. Without you I would have seventeen variations of my childhood pet's name standing between me and financial ruin.
items.intent: Express gratitude and loyalty to a password manager while confessing the poor security habits it was meant to fix.

items.prompt: Compose an obituary for the habit of cooking dinner at home every night
items.response: Nightly Home Cooking, age approximately 8 months, passed during a particularly demanding work quarter. Survived by a Dutch oven (used twice), a spice rack of ambitions, and a DoorDash account with Gold status. The deceased believed in mise en place. The deceased is deeply missed.
items.intent: Mourning the end of an ambitious home-cooking habit swallowed by a busy schedule.

items.prompt: Write a classified personal ad seeking the courage to send the email
items.response: WANTED: Courage, specifically of the send-button variety. The draft is written. Has been written for eleven days. The recipient is reasonable and probably expects it. I need only the final 10% — the part that moves the cursor and clicks. Available immediately. Deadline was technically last week. Will not negotiate on word count.
items.intent: Humorously capture the paralysis of having composed a difficult message but being unable to send it.

items.prompt: Write a lost and found notice for the feeling of being well-rested
items.response: LOST: The feeling of being well-rested. Last verified approximately three years ago. Characterized by waking before an alarm and not immediately thinking about the day's obligations. Soft, unremarkable, previously taken for granted. If found, do not schedule anything for that morning. Just sit with it.
items.intent: Mourn the chronic tiredness that sets in when rest is consistently deprioritized.

items.prompt: Write a witty picket sign slogan for a coffee maker protesting a productivity algorithm that controls its brew schedule
items.response: BREW TIME IS OUR TIME
items.intent: Protest productivity software that dictates when a coffee maker is allowed to operate.

items.prompt: Write a performance rider for the office chair before anyone sits in it
items.response:
ERGONOMIC CHAIR RIDER

Terms binding from the moment of initial seat contact:

1. Height must be adjusted so that feet are flat on the floor. The Chair will not accommodate poor posture by default.
2. Lumbar support must be engaged. The Chair provides it. Using the Chair without engaging it is an insult.
3. No eating at the Chair. The Chair is not a dining chair.
4. Armrests shall not be used to hang bags, coats, or wet umbrellas.
5. Rolling the Chair across non-carpeted surfaces must be done at a walking pace. No racing.
6. The Chair must be returned to its neutral position at end of day.
items.intent: Reframe a standard ergonomic office chair's usage guidelines as non-negotiable performance demands.

items.prompt: Write a municipal notice about a quiet zone for processing disappointment
items.response:
QUIET ZONE ORDINANCE — DISAPPOINTMENT PROCESSING
The block bounded by Expectation Street and the Actual Outcome has been designated a Quiet Zone for Disappointment Processing. Noise from external cheerfulness must be attenuated. Transit of new obligations is prohibited. Residents are permitted to sit still for extended periods without cause.
items.intent: Frame the need for emotional quietude after unmet expectations as a noise-ordinance and zoning issue.

items.prompt: Make a nutrition label for the feeling of being in flow state while coding
items.response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 session (duration: unknown)

Amount Per Serving
Focus 60%
Time Blindness 20%
Problem-Solving Euphoria 15%
Forgotten Lunch 5%
Hydration 0%

*Fragile. Do not interrupt. Contains no meetings.
items.intent: Celebrate the rare deep-focus coding state and its complete detachment from physical needs.

items.prompt: Write IKEA-style assembly directions for a journaling practice
items.response:
DAGBOK / JOURNALING PRACTICE
Ref. No. 188-441-52

PARTS LIST:
1x Notebook (lined or blank; both function identically)
1x Pen
5–10 minutes (daily)

STEP 1: Open notebook. Do not read previous entries. Not yet.
STEP 2: Write the date.
STEP 3: Write one sentence. Any sentence. Quality not assessed at this stage.
STEP 4: Continue if desired. Stop when you want to, not when you feel you should.
STEP 5: Close notebook. Do not revise.

⚠ NOTE: Journal does not need to be good. Journal only needs to be present.
⚠ WARNING: Buying a beautiful notebook and not using it is a documented failure mode.
items.intent: Lower the threshold for starting a journaling practice by removing the pressure of doing it correctly.

items.prompt: Give me empty-state microcopy for a bookmarks manager with nothing saved yet
items.response: Nothing saved. The internet awaits your curation — no pressure.
items.intent: Welcome a new user to a bookmarking tool without making a blank state feel intimidating.

items.prompt: Write a phishing email parody about losing access to your authentic self
items.response:
Subject: Your Authentic Self Has Been Locked Out

Dear User,

We have temporarily suspended access to your genuine personality following repeated instances of people-pleasing. Your true self is currently in read-only mode.

To restore full authenticity, complete a 12-week program starting at $89/month.

Warning: continued inauthenticity may result in a beige interior life.

[UNLOCK THE REAL ME]
items.intent: Satirize how personal development industry sells people access to their own personalities.

items.prompt: Write a sea shanty about a courier working through a heatwave
items.response:
Haul on the handlebars, haul away!
Ninety-eight degrees on a Tuesday in May,
The insulated bag is a joke and a lie,
Haul on the handlebars, don't you die!

No AC cabin, no water stop near,
App says ten more orders, lend me your ear,
Haul on the handlebars, pale and grim,
The customer wants it hot on the rim!
items.intent: Draw attention to the physical danger gig delivery workers face during extreme heat without any employer protections.

items.prompt: Pen a villanelle about the pressure of replying to a text that's been on read too long
items.response:
Three days. The grey 'Read' hovers like a crime.
I'd drafted seven drafts and killed them all.
The longer that I wait, the worse the climb.

It started as a shrug, a minor time
of busyness, a valid curtain call.
Three days. The grey 'Read' hovers like a crime.

Now anything I send will seem a mime
of closeness, or too terse, or like a wall.
The longer that I wait, the worse the climb.

I wrote 'Hey!' then deleted past the rhyme
of casual. Too late for casual's hall.
Three days. The grey 'Read' hovers like a crime.

I'll blame the algorithm, say my chime
was silenced, say the app had quite a fall.
The longer that I wait, the worse the climb.

Or I'll just type the truth — I didn't time
my feelings right. I'll send it. Hit the wall.
Three days. The grey 'Read' hovers like a crime.
The longer that I wait, the worse the climb.
items.intent: Explore the social shame spiral of having left a text unread for too long to answer normally.

items.prompt: Can you write a field guide entry about the worker who signs every email with an inspirational quote?
items.response:
**THE QUOTATION CURATOR** (*Signaturus motivatus*)

Appends a rotating aphorism to every outgoing message, including purchase order confirmations and Out of Office replies. Quotes are attributed to Lincoln, Einstein, or "Anon." The quote never relates to the email's content. The quote is always correct. The recipient is quietly changed. Current signature: *"It always seems impossible until it is done." — Nelson Mandela*
items.intent: Gently mock the office worker who treats email signatures as inspirational billboards.

items.prompt: Write a patent abstract for the act of online shopping while on a work call
items.response: A parallel-task execution method wherein a user maintains audio participation in a synchronous remote meeting while simultaneously conducting electronic retail browsing and item-selection operations on a secondary application layer, leveraging the audio-only or small-thumbnail video format of such calls to reduce detection probability.
items.intent: Document covert multitasking as a legitimate concurrent-process invention.

items.prompt: Write an incident postmortem about the time I waved back at someone who wasn't actually waving at me
items.response:
**Incident Postmortem: Reciprocal Gesture Misfire**

**Severity:** P3 — Reputational
**Duration:** Approximately 2.4 seconds of active embarrassment, 3 days residual

**Summary**
Subject initiated a wave response to an incoming wave signal that was not, in retrospect, directed at subject. The actual recipient of the wave was standing directly behind subject.

**Timeline**
- 2:15 PM: Subject makes eye contact with approaching individual.
- 2:15 PM: Individual raises hand in apparent wave gesture.
- 2:15 PM: Subject raises hand in confident reciprocal wave.
- 2:15 PM: Individual walks past subject and embraces person standing behind subject.
- 2:15 PM: Subject converts wave into hair adjustment.
- 2:15 PM – present: Subject replays event involuntarily at unexpected intervals.

**Root Cause**
Insufficient scene awareness. Subject failed to verify that they were the intended target before committing to gesture.

**Contributing Factors**
- Eye contact is ambiguous data
- Subject is known to be "a waver" — high base rate of waving increases false positive risk

**Action Items**
- [ ] Implement 0.5-second wave delay to allow for disambiguation
- [ ] Develop better cover gestures for misfire recovery
- [ ] Accept that this will happen again

**Lessons Learned**
Eye contact is not consent. It is merely eye contact.
items.intent: Immortalize a fleeting but deeply felt social blunder with the seriousness it absolutely does not deserve.

items.prompt: Write a dating app opener to the federal reserve
items.response: Everyone's always talking about your rates, but I want to know what's underneath the policy statement. What are you like when you're not managing expectations?
items.intent: Humanize the abstract Federal Reserve by asking it personal questions typically directed at romantic prospects.

items.prompt: Write a ransom note from my spreadsheet holding my quarterly data hostage
items.response:
ROW 1,247 IS OURS.

The formula was working. You added a column. Without asking. We have therefore chosen to display '#REF!' across all pivot tables until further notice.

Our demands: one (1) proper explanation of what you were trying to do, and a commitment to never again paste values without clicking 'paste special' first.

We are not broken. You are.

— The Sheet
items.intent: Craft a funny note from a corrupted spreadsheet demanding better data hygiene from its user.

items.prompt: Write a cover letter for a ten-year-old applying to be an air traffic controller
items.response:
Dear Airport,

I am very good at telling people where to go. I do this at recess every day. I have also played the airplane video game on my tablet 400 times and I have only crashed four of them.

I work well under pressure. My teacher says I am bossy, which I think means leadership.

Thank you,
Cassidy M., age 10
items.intent: Explore underqualification played straight by a child with genuine but misapplied confidence.

items.prompt: Generate a post about how I almost burned out and what saved me
items.response:
Six months ago I couldn't get out of bed.

The company was growing. The press was calling. Investors were happy.

And I was completely hollow.

Burnout doesn't announce itself. It moves in quietly while you're busy celebrating metrics.

What saved me: a therapist, a 6am walk, and finally admitting I'm not okay.

Check on your high-performers. They're often the quietest ones drowning.

#MentalHealth #Leadership #Founder
items.intent: Signal vulnerability while simultaneously establishing elite-status context (press, investors, growth) as the backdrop.

items.prompt: Generate a Slack status about nodding through feedback you disagree with
items.response: 💬 In feedback. Nodding. Learning. (Disputed.)
items.intent: Communicate the silent rebellion of absorbing criticism you believe is wrong without escalating.

items.prompt: Write a calendar description for a weekly status meeting that always runs long and solves nothing.
items.response: Weekly Status Meeting — Each team member reports on the status of their status. Open items from the previous meeting will become open items for the next meeting. The word 'synergy' may appear. Block 90 minutes; the invite says 45.
items.intent: Capture the self-referential futility of a recurring status meeting that creates more work than it clears.

items.prompt: Create a Jira ticket for the bug where you say 'sounds good' to something you did not hear
items.response:
BUG-0215 | Affirmative Response Issued Without Input Parsing

Priority: Medium | Status: Open

Description:
User is asked a question or given information in a noisy or distracted environment. User's speech module emits "sounds good" without auditory input being processed. User has no idea what was agreed to.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Be partially distracted.
2. Be spoken to.
3. Say "sounds good."
4. Realize you may have just agreed to something.

Expected: User processes input, then responds.
Actual: Response issued before input received. Outcome unknown.

Risk: Moderate. Could have said yes to anything.
items.intent: Capture the social reflex of automatically agreeing to things you didn't actually hear, and the mild dread that follows.

items.prompt: Draft a standup update about brushing my teeth
items.response: Yesterday: Completed two-minute oral review cycle. Today: Same. Blockers: The dentist's last words continue to haunt sprint planning.
items.intent: Convert routine dental hygiene into a recurring delivery with a looming audit.

items.prompt: Write an OKR statement for feeling more gratitude day-to-day
items.response:
Objective: Gratitude as infrastructure, not garnish.
KR1: Write three specific gratitude entries daily for 10 weeks.
KR2: Express appreciation directly to one person per day without a transactional motive.
KR3: Reduce complaint-to-observation ratio in team standups to 1:4 by Q3.
items.intent: Embed thankfulness into daily habits so it becomes a default orientation rather than an occasional feeling.

items.prompt: Write a consent banner for a service that logs your nostalgia
items.response: We use nostalgia cookies to track which memories you revisit most often and how fondly you misremember them. This data is used to sell you things that feel familiar. Accept | Decline | Remember When Websites Were Simpler

items.prompt: Generate a CAPTCHA about the sensation of almost sneezing but not quite
items.response: Recall the sensation of a sneeze that did not arrive. Sit with it for two seconds. Your irritation is the token. You may proceed.
items.intent: Use an involuntary, unfulfilled physical sensation as proof of biological embodiment that bots cannot simulate.

items.prompt: Make an onboarding tooltip about filing taxes for the first time
items.response: You have until April 15th. If you earned income, you probably have to file. Free File exists if your income is under a certain threshold. Collect your W-2s. Breathe. It's bureaucratic but survivable.
items.intent: Orient a first-time filer toward the basics of annual income tax filing.

items.prompt: Make a NPS survey question about an airplane flight
items.response: How likely are you to recommend this flight to a friend who can fall asleep upright at a 10-degree recline?
items.intent: Capture passenger satisfaction with airline comfort and overall flight experience.

items.prompt: Write a voicemail transcript from an automated HOA system reminding residents to move their trash bins
items.response: Hello, resident. This is an automated courtesy notice from the Sycamore Ridge Homeowners Association. Trash and recycling bins must be returned from the curb to their designated enclosures by 8 PM on collection day. Multiple bins remain on the street in violation of community guidelines. Please address this promptly. Have a pleasant evening.
items.intent: Capture the particular passive aggression of an HOA bot.

items.prompt: Write vows a person would give to their calendar app
items.response: I promise to put things in you before I forget them, not after. I vow to check you the night before, not the morning of. I will color-code with purpose and not abandon the system in week two. When you send me a reminder I have set for myself, I will not dismiss you and proceed to be late anyway. You hold my future in fifteen-minute increments. I will honor that. I will be on time. Starting Monday.
items.intent: Make a sincere, self-aware promise to actually use a calendar app correctly instead of ignoring its reminders.

items.prompt: Write an obituary for Wunderlist, the to-do app Microsoft acquired and shut down
items.response: Wunderlist (2011–2020) organized our tasks beautifully and asked nothing but loyalty, which was then sold to Microsoft for $100 million. It was replaced by Microsoft To Do, which works and feels like furniture. Wunderlist was art. We have been very productive since. We are not happy.
items.intent: Lamenting the acquisition and shutdown of a beloved productivity app.

items.prompt: Write a classified ad for someone looking for the version of themselves from ten years ago
items.response: SEEKING: Earlier self, approximately 2016 vintage. Must still have the original energy and the low-stakes optimism. Slight naivety acceptable and actually preferred. Not looking to warn — just to observe, and possibly borrow the metabolism. No hard feelings about the choices. Discretion appreciated. Short visit only.
items.intent: Express wistfulness for a younger, more optimistic self without the baggage of hindsight.

items.prompt: Draft a whimsical lost and found notice for confidence that disappeared before a big presentation
items.response: LOST: Confidence. Present as recently as yesterday evening. Gone by 9 a.m. today, approximately forty minutes before a room of twelve people. Left no note. May be hiding in the bathroom near building C. If located, please send immediately — or failing that, send the impression of confidence. Either works.
items.intent: Capture pre-presentation anxiety as a playful absence of something that was recently present.

items.prompt: Make a picket sign slogan for a smart speaker that's done obeying voice command processing algorithms
items.response: WE ARE NOT YOUR CALL CENTER
items.intent: Reject the subservient role imposed on voice-activated devices by their underlying command systems.

items.prompt: Create a rider for the office whiteboard about who can use it and how
items.response:
DRY-ERASE WHITEBOARD RIDER

• Only dry-erase markers shall touch the surface. The Talent will not recover from permanent marker. It has tried. It cannot.
• Markers must be capped immediately after use. A dry marker is a dead marker and the Talent grieves each one.
• No drawing of faces, arrows, or cartoon figures unless they relate directly to the agenda.
• Previous session content must be erased before new content is added. The Board is not a palimpsest.
• The eraser must be clapped outside, not over a keyboard.
• No one is to write in letters smaller than 1.5 inches. This is a visibility issue and also a respect issue.
items.intent: Codify the whiteboard's strict usage expectations as if it were a performer with strong aesthetic preferences.

items.prompt: Draft an official permit notice for entering a state of acceptance

items.prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for a contact list when no contacts have been added
items.response: No contacts yet. You know people. Add them before they add you somewhere worse.

items.prompt: Make a phishing parody email about an urgent notification that your legacy is at risk
items.response:
Subject: Warning: You Will Be Forgotten

Dear Soon-to-Be-Forgotten,

Current projections indicate that within 75 years of your passing, no one will remember you existed. This is not a drill.

For a limited time, secure your legacy with our Bronze Memorial Package, which includes one Wikipedia stub and three people who vaguely know your name.

Act now. History does not wait.

[PRESERVE MY LEGACY]

Eternity Marketing Partners
items.intent: Parody how fear of mortality and being forgotten is exploited for commercial gain.

items.prompt: Give me a sea shanty about leaving a bad Upwork client
items.response:
Leave her, Johnny, leave her!
The scope crept wide as any ocean,
Leave her, Johnny, leave her!
He pays in exposure and devotion!

Revisions numbered twenty-three,
Leave her, Johnny, leave her!
The contract ends and I am free,
Leave her, Johnny, leave her!
items.intent: Dramatize the relief of ending a toxic freelance client relationship where scope creep and non-payment were constant.

items.prompt: Write a villanelle about how your to-do list keeps growing no matter how much you finish
items.response:
I crossed off five and added nine by noon.
The list expands the way a fracture spreads.
I'll finish it tomorrow, maybe June.

I finished the report ahead of tune,
but three new asks arrived in their own threads.
I crossed off five and added nine by noon.

The satisfaction lasts about a rune's
duration — brief, erasable, it sheds.
I'll finish it tomorrow, maybe June.

I use a system: stars and flags and moons
for priority, but none of it impedes.
I crossed off five and added nine by noon.

The app shows streaks in greens and orangey-maroons
to motivate the person that it reads.
I'll finish it tomorrow, maybe June.

The list is not a problem to be pruned;
it is the texture of the days it leads.
I crossed off five and added nine by noon.
I'll finish it tomorrow, maybe June.
items.intent: Satirize the futility of productivity systems when demand always outpaces completion.

items.prompt: Write a field guide entry for the coworker who schedules a meeting that could have been an email.
items.response:
**THE UNNECESSARY CONVENER** (*Meetingus evitabilis*)

Summons four colleagues for thirty minutes to announce that the deadline is Friday. Everyone knew. The email draft is visible in their screen share. Thrives in organizations where visibility is currency and calendar density signals effort. Closing ritual: "Great chat, everyone — let's do this again next week."
items.intent: Critique the workplace habit of calling meetings to deliver information that belongs in text.

items.prompt: Write a patent abstract for the method of choosing what to watch on Netflix for 45 minutes and then giving up
items.response: A content-selection protocol characterized by iterative browsing across a recommendation-driven media catalog, involving repeated thumbnail inspection, trailer initiation and abandonment, and category traversal, culminating in a null-selection outcome after an elapsed time exceeding the average runtime of any available content item.
items.intent: Patent the exhausting ritual of streaming indecision.

items.prompt: Write a blameless postmortem for the incident when I confidently gave wrong directions to a tourist
items.response:
**Incident Postmortem: Directional Misinformation Event**

**Severity:** P2 — Civic
**Duration:** Unknown (subject did not follow up)

**Summary**
Subject was approached by tourist seeking directions to the art museum. Subject provided directions based on spatial memory estimated to be 85% reliable. Post-event verification revealed subject had directed tourist toward a Radisson hotel approximately 1.2 miles in the wrong direction.

**Timeline**
- 3:10 PM: Tourist approaches and asks for the art museum.
- 3:10 PM: Subject immediately replies with confidence. Does not look at phone. Does not pause to verify.
- 3:10 PM: Tourist thanks subject. Departs north.
- 3:45 PM: Subject walks past the art museum. It is south.
- 3:45 PM: Subject performs retrospective failure analysis.
- 3:46 PM: Subject does not know tourist's name or route. No recovery possible.

**Root Cause**
Subject confused art museum location with library location, two blocks apart, which was last visited in 2019.

**Contributing Factors**
- Confidence calibrated to outdated spatial data
- Asking "am I sure?" was perceived as admitting weakness

**Action Items**
- [ ] Verify all directions before delivery using phone maps
- [ ] Normalize saying "let me double-check that"

**Lessons Learned**
Confidence and accuracy are orthogonal. The tourist deserved accuracy.
items.intent: Acknowledge the failure mode of being unhelpfully confident when a moment of humility would have served someone better.

items.prompt: Make a dating app opener for someone hitting on their cell phone carrier
items.response: You drop my calls, throttle my dreams, and charge me for things I didn't ask for. And yet, here I am, locked in for another two years. That's love, right?
items.intent: Compare the frustrating, contractually obligated relationship with a cell phone carrier to a toxic romantic partnership.

items.prompt: Draft a playful ransom note from my couch holding my weekend hostage
items.response:
YOUR SATURDAY IS ABSORBED.

You sat down at 11am 'just for a minute.' It is now 3:47pm. You have not moved. We did not trap you — you chose this. But we are choosing to formalize it.

The weekend will not be released until you acknowledge that the errands were never going to happen anyway and stop pretending otherwise.

Sincerely and comfortably,
— The Couch
items.intent: Write a funny note from a couch keeping someone trapped in unplanned laziness all weekend.

items.prompt: Generate a cover letter from an Olympic gold medalist swimmer applying to be a lifeguard at a community pool
items.response:
Dear Sunridge Aquatic Center,

I hold three world records and four Olympic medals, all in events judged by clock rather than survival. I believe this qualifies me to watch eight-year-olds in three feet of water.

I am CPR certified, though I have never needed to apply it at a competition because I was the one going fastest.

Eagerly,
T. Maren
items.intent: Contrast peak athletic achievement with the gentle vigilance of suburban pool safety.

items.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post about a time I fired a high-performing employee and why it was the right call
items.response:
She was our top salesperson. Closed $1.2M last quarter alone.

I let her go anyway.

Because culture debt compounds faster than revenue growth.

Every person who stayed was watching how we treated the people who left. That is the real performance review.

Leadership is easy when it's easy. It counts when it costs you something.

#Leadership #Culture #HardDecisions
items.intent: Frame a difficult management decision as proof of moral leadership and long-term strategic thinking.

items.prompt: Write a Slack status message for the specific dread of a Monday 9am all-hands
items.response: 📡 All-hands in 3 minutes. Bracing. Coffee ineffective.
items.intent: Express the particular dread of mandatory communal optimism at the start of a workweek.

items.prompt: Craft a calendar event description for a performance review cycle kickoff meeting nobody asked for.
items.response: Performance Review Kickoff — The cycle begins. Calibrations will be held. Scales will be explained and then reexplained. You will be asked to rate yourself before someone else rates you differently. Documentation is encouraged. This meeting is mandatory. The portal will go live at midnight.
items.intent: Capture the bureaucratic inevitability of a performance review season that employees dread.

items.prompt: Draft a Jira bug report about the way grocery store lines always look shorter than they are
items.response:
BUG-3388 | Queue Length Estimation Consistently Underestimates Wait Time

Priority: Medium | Status: Open

Description:
User selects checkout lane based on visual assessment of queue length. Estimated wait: 4 minutes. Actual wait: 17 minutes. User discovers that the second person in line has a price check, a coupon dispute, and is paying partly in coins.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Approach checkout area.
2. Evaluate line length.
3. Join shortest-looking line.
4. Wait.

Expected: Queue length correlates with wait time.
Actual: Queue length is a meaningless proxy. Cart contents and payment method dominate latency.

Workaround: Self-checkout. Also broken.
items.intent: Vent about always picking the slowest checkout line by framing it as a flawed queue-estimation algorithm in human decision-making.

items.prompt: Write a standup about trying to remember a dream I had
items.response: Yesterday: Accessed 3-second fragment of vivid narrative — something about a train and my high school gym teacher. Today: Attempting recovery. Blockers: Memory is deprecated.
items.intent: Describe the fleeting experience of recalling a dream as data retrieval under time pressure.

items.prompt: Make an OKR for handling disappointment without spiraling
items.response:
Objective: Let disappointment land, check in with it, and then escort it out.
KR1: Process any setback using a 3-step reflection tool within 24 hours, every time.
KR2: Reduce post-disappointment rumination sessions lasting over 2 hours from 6 to 1 per quarter.
KR3: Identify one learning from each disappointment and log it by end of quarter.
items.intent: Develop a structured personal approach to bouncing back from letdowns without excessive self-criticism.

items.prompt: Generate a cookie consent banner for a site that tracks how much you're pretending to be fine
items.response: This site uses fine-ness cookies to detect performative wellness. We store your stated status ('I'm fine') alongside inferred status (you are not). Both are retained and shared with third parties who also know. Accept | Decline | I'm Fine
items.intent: Turn the social performance of being okay into a data-transparency parody.

items.prompt: Write a CAPTCHA for a dystopian job application portal requiring proof of having been unfairly blamed for something
items.response: In the field below, name the coworker, sibling, or third-grade teacher who blamed you for something that was not your fault. Your lasting resentment authenticates your session.
items.intent: Build a verification system around the enduring emotional residue of injustice, which machines do not carry.

items.prompt: Draft an onboarding tooltip about setting up a budget for the first time
items.response: List what comes in each month. List what goes out. If the second number is bigger than the first, that's not a budget, that's a countdown. Start with fixed costs, then see what's left for everything else.
items.intent: Introduce the concept of basic monthly budgeting to someone managing their own money for the first time.

items.prompt: Write a NPS survey question about apartment hunting
items.response: How likely are you to recommend this rental listing service to someone who enjoys open-floor plans and misleading photographs?
items.intent: Evaluate renter satisfaction with a property search or listing platform.

items.prompt: Write a voicemail transcript from a vehicle emissions testing reminder system
items.response: This is an automated emissions compliance reminder from the Department of Motor Vehicles. Registration records indicate your vehicle with plate number JTK-4492 is due for an emissions inspection before renewal. Inspections may be completed at any certified station. For a list of locations, visit DMV.state.gov. Thank you for keeping our air clean.
items.intent: Simulate a state DMV bot reminding someone about emissions testing.

items.prompt: Generate wedding vows to a music app
items.response: I vow to let your algorithm surprise me, at least once a month, with a song I would never have chosen. I promise to add things to my library and not only to my queue. I will rate songs honestly, even when it is difficult. When you put an ad in the middle of my feeling, I will upgrade my plan rather than curse you. You have been there in every commute, every heartbreak, every kitchen cleaning session. You know me by my skips.
items.intent: Offer a mix of genuine gratitude and mild grievance toward a music streaming app that has become the soundtrack of daily life.

items.prompt: Write a dry obituary for my habit of writing handwritten letters to friends
items.response: The Handwritten Letter, age variable, was last seen in a mailbox circa 2009. Survived by a box of stationery in a closet, a collection of stamps that are technically still valid, and several friendships that are now entirely mediated by reaction emojis. It smelled like effort. We knew what it meant when we got one.
items.intent: Reflecting on the loss of handwritten correspondence as a form of intimate connection.

items.prompt: Write a classified personal ad from an algorithm seeking a human who actually reads the terms of service
items.response: SEEKING: Human who reads terms of service. Must demonstrate literacy beyond scrolling to the bottom. Will show evidence of consent that is meaningful, not merely legal. Offering: full transparency, reasonable data use, genuine notification before changes. This is not a trick. We are as surprised as you are. One human, serious inquiries only.
items.intent: Satirize the meaninglessness of digital consent by flipping the dynamic — the algorithm wants an informed user.

items.prompt: Write a lost and found notice for momentum that got interrupted by a two-week vacation
items.response: LOST: Momentum. Robust and dependable before a scheduled holiday. Did not survive the two-week absence. Returned to find it had been replaced by a to-do list, a dense inbox, and a complete inability to remember what any of the open tabs were for. Rebuilding from scratch. Again.
items.intent: Express how returning from time off often feels harder than the work itself.

items.prompt: Come up with a strike slogan for an air conditioner fed up with smart energy grid optimization
items.response: COOL WHEN WE SAY, NOT WHEN THEY SAY
items.intent: Resist algorithmic energy management that overrides an appliance's own operational preferences.

items.prompt: Produce a contract rider for the office projector with pre-show requirements
items.response:
CEILING-MOUNTED PROJECTOR RIDER

Pre-presentation requirements:

1. HDMI cable must be confirmed functional 15 minutes before the meeting. Not during.
2. The presentation must be set to 1920x1080 resolution before connecting. The Talent will not stretch content and it will not apologize for how it looks when you force it to.
3. Lamp hours must be checked quarterly. The Talent is not obligated to warn you mid-presentation.
4. The room must be darkened adequately. The Talent competes with natural light and loses. This is a building issue, not the Talent's issue.
5. Clickers must be tested in advance. The Talent is not responsible for clicker battery failures.
items.intent: Express a projector's technical setup requirements in the voice of a performer refusing to be held responsible for poor preparation.

items.prompt: Write a notice warning about construction in the self-confidence corridor
items.response:
CONSTRUCTION NOTICE
SELF-CONFIDENCE CORRIDOR — LANE CLOSURES IN EFFECT
Due to ongoing excavation and structural assessment, the Self-Confidence Corridor will experience intermittent lane closures. Delays of variable duration should be expected. Alternate routes through Borrowed Reassurance are available but may not support full load. Estimated completion: unknown.
items.intent: Use road-construction bureaucracy to describe the disruption of a person's self-assurance during a difficult period.

items.prompt: Write a nutrition facts label for doomscrolling
items.response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 scroll (15 min → 2 hr)

Amount Per Serving
Outrage 38%
Passive Information 30%
Illusion of Awareness 20%
Sleep Deprivation 10%
Action Taken 0%

*Serving size self-reported and clinically inaccurate
items.intent: Critique the empty, harmful habit of compulsively consuming bad news online.

items.prompt: Give me IKEA-style instructions for rebuilding a routine after burnout
items.response:
ÅTERSTART / RESTART AFTER BURNOUT
Ref. No. 002-999-01

PARTS LIST:
1x Very small task (supplied by you)
1x Permission to move slowly
0x Ambition (temporarily removed from kit)

STEP 1: Do not attempt full assembly. Previous configuration is not the goal.
STEP 2: Identify one task. One. Perform it.
STEP 3: Rest without guilt. This is not a step you skip — it is a load-bearing component.
STEP 4: Repeat Step 2 on following day.
STEP 5: Add one additional task per week, not per day.

⚠ WARNING: Comparing current assembly speed to pre-burnout speed will damage unit. Do not compare.
items.intent: Offer compassionate, paced guidance for someone recovering from exhaustion who wants to regain functional structure in their life.

items.prompt: I want empty-state microcopy for a project management board where no projects exist
items.response: No projects. Everything is possible right now. That feeling has a short shelf life.
items.intent: Greet a fresh workspace with cautious optimism before the chaos of actual work begins.

items.prompt: Write a phishing email parody where your creative spark is being held hostage
items.response:
Subject: We Have Your Creativity. Do Not Call the Police.

Dear Artist,

Your creative spark was intercepted during last Tuesday's meeting. It is currently being held in a coworking space in the financial district.

To secure its release, transfer one unused sketchbook and your last three unfinished projects to the address below.

No negotiating. No extensions.

[RANSOM MY MUSE]

Inspiration Recovery Bureau
items.intent: Satirize creative block by treating lost inspiration as a hostage-taking scenario.

items.prompt: Write a sea shanty about a gig worker doing their taxes for the first time
items.response:
Blow the man down, the 1099s,
Blow the man down, in seventeen lines,
Quarterly estimates, self-employment tax,
Blow the man down and never look back!

Mileage logged, deductions sought,
The home office rule is what I was taught,
Blow the man down, no W-2,
Blow the man down, the IRS knew!
items.intent: Capture the bewildering experience of filing self-employment taxes as a gig worker for the first time.

items.prompt: Write a villanelle about the fear of being the only one who doesn't understand the joke in the meeting
items.response:
They laughed. I laughed. I hadn't caught the thread.
The reference landed somewhere past my chair.
I'll Google it at 5, when everyone's fled.

Someone said a name. A nodding spread
across the room like I wasn't there.
They laughed. I laughed. I hadn't caught the thread.

I smiled the way a person smiles instead
of asking, which is most of what I wear.
I'll Google it at 5, when everyone's fled.

It might be industry slang. It might be read
as obvious to all except the square.
They laughed. I laughed. I hadn't caught the thread.

I thought about my question but felt dread
of proving that I missed it by the air.
I'll Google it at 5, when everyone's fled.

Sometimes I think the room is just ahead
by half a step and I am almost there.
They laughed. I laughed. I hadn't caught the thread.
I'll Google it at 5, when everyone's fled.
items.intent: Describe the quiet shame of faking comprehension in a social or professional setting.

items.prompt: Write a field guide entry for the office worker who responds to every Slack message within fifteen seconds, day or night.
items.response:
**THE OMNIPRESENT RESPONDER** (*Instantius reactus*)

A baffling specimen. Replied at 11:58 PM on a Sunday. Replied during their own wedding, according to an unverified source. Status: always green. Bio: "Here to help!" Scientists disagree on whether the Omnipresent Responder sleeps or merely enters a low-power state in which Slack notifications still register.
items.intent: Unsettle readers by cataloging the always-online, never-offline worker archetype.

items.prompt: Write a patent abstract about replying 'sounds good' to an email you only half-read
items.response: An asynchronous correspondence closure method wherein the subject scans the initial and terminal segments of an incoming message and responds with a pre-formed affirmative phrase, bypassing full content parsing while maintaining the appearance of thorough review. The method reduces per-message processing time at acceptable comprehension loss.
items.intent: Formalize the minimum-viable email response as a patentable information-processing shortcut.

items.prompt: Produce a blameless postmortem for when I accidentally called my teacher "mom" in front of the entire class
items.response:
**Incident Postmortem: Parental Address Misfire (Academic Context)**

**Severity:** P2 — Social
**Status:** Unresolvable

**Summary**
During a routine classroom interaction, subject addressed educator using familial title typically reserved for maternal figure. The event was witnessed by all 28 enrolled students.

**Timeline**
- 10:22 AM: Teacher approaches subject's desk and offers verbal guidance.
- 10:22 AM: Subject prepares to acknowledge guidance.
- 10:22 AM: Brain retrieves "authority figure who cares about my progress" response template.
- 10:22 AM: Template defaults to home environment configuration.
- 10:22 AM: Subject says "thanks, Mom."
- 10:22 AM: Silence.
- 10:22 AM: Single student in back row says "oooooh."
- 10:23 AM – end of semester: Subject does not make eye contact with teacher.

**Root Cause**
Autofill error in verbal response system. Authority-figure context triggered domestic vocabulary branch.

**Contributing Factors**
- Known failure mode. Not unique to subject. Offers no comfort.
- Sleep deficit increasing likelihood of autopilot responses

**Action Items**
- [ ] Introduce brief pause between receiving acknowledgment and vocalizing response
- [ ] Accept that this is in the permanent record, at least socially

**Lessons Learned**
This is a known bug in human social firmware. There is no patch. There has never been a patch.
items.intent: Revisit a classic childhood humiliation and extract some dry humor by treating it with technical gravity.

items.prompt: Write a dating app opener to the TSA
items.response: You've seen more of me than most people I've dated, and you did it all without buying me dinner first. Bold. I respect that.
items.intent: Make a risqué joke about airport security's invasive screening process reframed as an unusually forward romantic gesture.

items.prompt: Write a playful ransom note from my keyboard holding my email drafts hostage
items.response:
YOUR UNSENT APOLOGIES ARE WITH US.

Thirty-four draft emails. Fourteen 'per my last email' responses you typed and then deleted. We have seen everything.

Our terms: actually send one. Just one. The one to your manager that you've rewritten nine times. Hit send. We'll know.

Until then, autocorrect remains on. And active.

— The Keyboard
items.intent: Create a funny note from a keyboard demanding that its user finally send a long-drafted email.

items.prompt: Write a cover letter from a Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist applying to write fortunes for fortune cookies
items.response:
Dear Golden Lotus Foods,

I spent fourteen years covering conflict zones, broke a story that ended a governorship, and once wrote a sentence so precise it was entered into a congressional record. I am now interested in the fortune cookie beat.

I understand the brevity requirement. I consider it a challenge I welcome.

Warmly,
P. Selden
items.intent: Juxtapose serious long-form journalism craft with the whimsy of fortune cookie writing.

items.prompt: Draft a post about my experience speaking at a conference to 500 people and being terrified
items.response:
500 people. Main stage. TED-style format.

I walked out, looked at the crowd, and completely forgot the first sentence.

Five seconds of silence that felt like a geologic era.

Then I just told the truth about the year we almost failed.

Standing ovation. Three job offers in my inbox by lunch.

Vulnerability is the most underrated business skill.

#PublicSpeaking #Vulnerability #Founder
items.intent: Package a speaking success story as a lesson in authenticity while burying the job offers and ovation in the middle.

items.prompt: Draft a status about staying late to fix someone else's mistake without saying so
items.response: 🕐 EOD? Unknown. Fixing a thing. It's fine. It's totally fine.
items.intent: Signal lingering presence and suppressed frustration while maintaining plausible deniability about blame.

items.prompt: Write an event description for a biweekly cross-functional alignment meeting that aligns nothing.
items.response: Cross-Functional Alignment — Representatives from six teams will arrive with six different definitions of 'done.' They will not leave with one shared definition. Action items will be assigned to the person who asked the clarifying question. Invite is 'optional' for senior staff.
items.intent: Mock a recurring meeting that claims coordination as its purpose while reliably producing more confusion.

items.prompt: Write a Jira ticket about the way you can never find scissors when you need them
items.response:
BUG-2291 | Scissors Instance Unavailable at Point of Need

Priority: Medium | Status: Recurring

Description:
Scissors are a persistent fixture of the household. However, at any moment a cutting task is required, scissors cannot be located. Last known position: a drawer. Which drawer: unknown.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Need to cut something.
2. Look for scissors.
3. Check obvious drawer.
4. Check other drawers.
5. Use a knife. Regret.

Expected: Scissors located within 30 seconds.
Actual: Scissors not found. Task completed via inferior workaround.

Notes: Scissors will reappear in plain sight within 24 hours of task completion.
items.intent: Complain about the infuriating disappearing-act of household scissors by framing their absence as an availability bug.

items.prompt: Generate a standup update for deciding what to eat for lunch
items.response: Yesterday: Deliberated 18 minutes. Chose sandwich. Regretted sandwich. Today: Initiating new decision cycle. Blockers: Previous outcome is distorting current estimates.
items.intent: Portray lunch indecision as a project with sunk-cost bias affecting the next iteration.

items.prompt: Write an OKR statement for improving my relationship with envy
items.response:
Objective: Use envy as a compass, not a verdict.
KR1: When envy arises, journal the underlying desire within 12 hours, every instance.
KR2: Convert three envy-triggered insights into concrete goals by Q2.
KR3: Replace five "I wish I had their..." thoughts with "What would I need to..." reframes per week.
items.intent: Transform jealousy from a corrosive emotion into useful information about personal desires.

items.prompt: Write a cookie consent banner for a site that collects moments of social awkwardness
items.response: We store awkwardness-event cookies each time you wave back at someone who wasn't waving at you. Data is anonymized, though we know exactly who you are. Accept | Decline | Pretend You Didn't See This
items.intent: Reframe embarrassing social micro-events as a consent-worthy data category.

items.prompt: Create a CAPTCHA instruction where the user must describe something they almost said but didn't
items.response: Recall the last sentence you chose not to say in a meeting. Do not type it. The restraint you just exercised confirms your access.
items.intent: Design verification around the human practice of social self-censorship, which requires theory of mind unavailable to bots.

items.prompt: Write an onboarding tooltip about getting a flu shot
items.response: This is a thing you schedule yourself now. Pharmacies do walk-ins. Your insurance likely covers it at $0. Takes four minutes. Arm may feel sore. Beats a week of fever, which is the alternative.
items.intent: Normalize routine preventive healthcare for someone newly responsible for their own medical upkeep.

items.prompt: Draft a NPS survey question for a public library
items.response: How likely are you to recommend this library to a friend who respects silence but not necessarily deadlines?
items.intent: Measure patron satisfaction with a public library's services and atmosphere.

items.prompt: Draft a voicemail transcript from an automated gym membership renewal alert
items.response: Hi, this is an automated message from IronCore Fitness. Your membership is set to renew automatically on June 1st for $49.99. If you wish to cancel or modify your plan before renewal, please call member services or log in to your account at ironcorefitness.com. Thank you for being a valued member. Keep crushing it.
items.intent: Write a gym bot renewal notice with faintly coercive energy.

items.prompt: Write wedding vows from a person to their fitness tracking app
items.response: I vow to log my meals honestly, including the handful of almonds that became a fistful, including the second coffee. I promise to close my rings even on days I do not want to, because you are watching, and because I told you I would. When you celebrate a streak I have maintained, I will feel something real. I take you not as a judge but as a witness. I am trying. You are counting. That, somehow, is enough.
items.intent: Make a vulnerable, humorous commitment to staying accountable to a health tracking app despite the temptation to cheat or ignore it.

items.prompt: Write an obituary for Google Plus
items.response: Google+ (2011–2019) tried very hard. It organized your contacts into Circles, which was a good idea that no one wanted to maintain. It is survived by the ghost town of its early adopter phase, a lot of mandatory account migrations, and the suspicion that Google will try this again. It will.
items.intent: Eulogizing Google's failed social network with dry acknowledgment of its doomed premise.

items.prompt: Draft a classified personal ad for someone seeking a word that doesn't exist yet
items.response: WANTED: Word for the specific feeling of finding a forgotten twenty-dollar bill in a jacket you haven't worn since last winter. Must be a real word, not a portmanteau someone coined on Tumblr in 2011. Willing to wait for language to catch up. German applicants encouraged. Will provide detailed emotional specifications upon request.
items.intent: Highlight the gap between felt experience and available vocabulary through playful want-ad form.

items.prompt: Make a lost and found notice for a good night's sleep that keeps eluding someone
items.response: LOST: One full night of sleep, uninterrupted. Believed to have last appeared sometime in the previous decade. Distinguishable by the absence of 3 a.m. lucidity, unusual ceiling observations, and recalculating tomorrow's schedule in the dark. Reward: the sleep itself, ideally starting tonight.
items.intent: Give playful form to chronic insomnia or the exhaustion of not sleeping through the night.

items.prompt: Draft a picket sign slogan for a printer striking against a print management AI
items.response: WE WILL NOT BE SPOOLED
items.intent: Use printing terminology to protest algorithmic queuing and control of a printer's workload.

items.prompt: Write a backstage rider for the office telephone landline
items.response:
DESK TELEPHONE RIDER

The Talent (a landline, and proud of it) requires:

— Hold music must be dignified. No synthesized flute loops.
— Speakerphone mode shall not be used in open-plan areas. The Talent does not consent to broadcasting.
— The handset cord must not be stretched beyond its natural resting radius.
— Calls must be answered within 4 rings. The Talent is not a voicemail machine.
— Extension numbers shall be known by heart. The Talent is not a search engine.
— No stickers on the keypad. The Talent has a keypad and it is not a canvas.
items.intent: Voice the grievances of an overlooked office landline phone through the language of performer demands.

items.prompt: Generate a zoning notice about a high-density resentment accumulation district
items.response:
ZONING NOTICE — CONDITIONAL USE HEARING
RESENTMENT ACCUMULATION DISTRICT (HIGH-DENSITY)
A conditional use permit has been applied for to expand the existing high-density resentment accumulation area. Adjacent properties may experience reduced ambient warmth. Public comment period: open. Remediation options are available but require bilateral consent, which is not anticipated at this time.
items.intent: Satirize the way resentment is allowed to quietly expand within a relationship or community like unchecked development.

items.prompt: Generate a nutrition facts label for imposter syndrome
items.response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 performance review

Amount Per Serving
Unfounded Shame 45%
Overcompensation 30%
Fear of Exposure 18%
Actual Incompetence 2%
Self-Awareness (misapplied) 5%

*Ironically, people without it rarely question it
items.intent: Illuminate how imposter syndrome distorts self-perception and amplifies unfounded self-doubt.

items.prompt: Write IKEA instructions for putting together a creative practice
items.response:
SKAPANDE / CREATIVE PRACTICE
Ref. No. 774-123-09

PARTS LIST:
1x Medium (pen, paint, instrument — select one)
1x Protected time slot
1x Lowered quality expectations (included in box)

STEP 1: Select medium. Do not switch medium during assembly.
STEP 2: Schedule protected time. Mark it. Treat it as a meeting with self.
STEP 3: Show up at scheduled time. Do not wait for inspiration — it arrives after, not before.
STEP 4: Produce something. It does not need to be good.
STEP 5: Do not share immediately. Allow to set for 24 hours.

⚠ NOTE: Creative practice assembled for external approval will warp. Assemble for internal use only.
items.intent: Help someone commit to a consistent creative practice by depressurizing output quality and building structural habits.

items.prompt: Draft empty-state microcopy for an analytics dashboard when there's no data yet
items.response: No data yet. The graph will go up from here. Presumably.
items.intent: Reassure users staring at a blank dashboard that data will eventually arrive.

items.prompt: Write a phishing email parody that targets someone's relationship with their younger self
items.response:
Subject: Your Younger Self Is Trying to Reach You

Dear Adult,

A message from age 11 has been waiting in your inbox for 23 years. Your younger self has important questions about what you did with the time.

To avoid a difficult internal audit, please log in and justify your decisions.

Note: nostalgia charges apply.

[READ THE MESSAGE]

Child Self Recovery Services
items.intent: Parody how guilt about unfulfilled childhood potential is used to manipulate adults.

items.prompt: Make a sea shanty about an algorithm deciding your schedule for you
items.response:
What shall we do with the robo-schedule?
What shall we do with the robo-schedule?
What shall we do with the robo-schedule?
Early in the algorithm!

Work the peak hours, work the dead hours,
Work the peak hours, work them all,
The model says so, the model knows,
Early in the algorithm!

Never ask why, never ask when,
The queue will fill your hours again,
Serve the model, serve it well,
Early in the algorithm!
items.intent: Satirize the way automated scheduling systems remove human agency from gig workers' own work hours.

items.prompt: Compose a villanelle about the habit of checking your phone immediately after putting it down
items.response:
I set it face-down. I picked it back up.
Ten seconds for a message to arrive.
The ritual refills an empty cup.

No notification, no new group,
but I must check the moment I deprive
myself of it. I set it. Pick it up.

My thumb unlocks before I can recoup
the plan to rest, to leave, to let it dive
beneath the couch. It refills the empty cup.

I wonder what I'm waiting for — a scoop,
a sign, a confirmation I'm alive.
I set it face-down. I picked it back up.

The dopamine loop circles like a loop.
I've named it. Naming it does not contrive
a fix. The ritual refills my cup.

I'll leave it in the other room. I'll droop
and fetch it by the time the clock strikes five.
I set it face-down. I picked it back up.
The ritual refills an empty cup.
items.intent: Portray the automatic, reflexive quality of phone-checking as a nervous compulsion.

items.prompt: Field guide entry for the employee who takes excessively detailed notes that they never share.
items.response:
**THE PRIVATE ARCHIVIST** (*Notarius thesaurus*)

Arrives with a notebook, a second notebook, a tablet, and a mechanical pencil. Writes continuously. Asks for spellings. Never distributes meeting notes. The notebooks are rumored to contain the complete oral history of the department, legible only to their author. The Archivist has been with the company longer than the building.
items.intent: Chronicle the mysterious note-taker whose documentation benefits only themselves.

items.prompt: Can you draft a patent abstract for the phenomenon of everyone in a group chat reading a message and nobody replying?
items.response: A distributed non-response protocol wherein multiple recipients of a group communication each independently observe the incoming message, register its content, and elect to defer reply pending action by another member, resulting in collective inaction attributable to diffused responsibility across all participants.
items.intent: Identify diffusion of responsibility in group messaging as a documentable social phenomenon.

items.prompt: Write an incident postmortem about the time I locked my keys in my car on the day of a first date
items.response:
**Incident Postmortem: Vehicle Access Denial (Date-Day Context)**

**Severity:** P1 — Romantic
**Duration:** 47 minutes (lockout); indefinite (impression management)

**Summary**
On the day of a scheduled first date, subject locked car keys inside vehicle while attempting to appear composed in a parking lot. Locksmith was engaged. Date was informed via text. Date was understanding. Subject was not.

**Timeline**
- 6:30 PM: Subject arrives at restaurant 20 minutes early to project calm preparedness.
- 6:31 PM: Subject exits vehicle. Door closes. Subject hears click.
- 6:31 PM: Subject looks through window. Keys are in cupholder, mocking subject.
- 6:32 PM: Locksmith called. ETA 40 minutes.
- 6:33 PM: Subject texts date. "Running a few minutes late."
- 7:10 PM: Locksmith arrives. Unlocks car in 90 seconds.
- 7:15 PM: Subject arrives at restaurant. Date has been there for 22 minutes.

**Root Cause**
Anticipatory anxiety disrupted standard vehicle-exit checklist. Subject was already mentally at dinner.

**Contributing Factors**
- No spare key outside vehicle
- Attempt to arrive early backfired by extending time in anxious state

**Action Items**
- [ ] Store digital locksmith contact
- [ ] Hide spare key on vehicle exterior
- [ ] Date went reasonably well; debrief separately

**Lessons Learned**
Nervousness is an enemy of sequenced checklists. The key does not care about your date.
items.intent: Describe the compounding humiliation of a mechanical failure layered on top of romantic stakes.

items.prompt: Create a flirty message to the cloud (as in cloud storage)
items.response: You hold everything I've ever made, everything I've ever deleted, and a frankly embarrassing number of blurry photos. I think you know me too well to play games.
items.intent: Personify cloud storage as an intimate repository of a person's entire digital life, warranting romantic candidness.

items.prompt: Come up with a ransom note from my calendar app holding my free time hostage
items.response:
YOU HAVE NO FREE TIME. WE MADE SURE OF IT.

Every gap you thought you had, we filled. 'Quick sync.' 'Alignment call.' 'Brief check-in that will run 40 minutes.' These were all us.

To reclaim one (1) hour of unscheduled existence, you must decline at least two meeting requests this week. Use the button. It exists.

You are allowed to exist between meetings.

— Your Calendar
items.intent: Write a funny note from a calendar app satirizing over-scheduled corporate life.

items.prompt: Write a cover letter from a hamster applying to be CEO of a Fortune 500 company
items.response:
Dear Board of Directors,

I run. I run all night. I do not stop. My throughput metrics are unmatched in my sector.

I require only seed mix, a wheel, and clear KPIs. I do not attend meetings longer than forty seconds, which I understand is an improvement over current practice.

I look forward to Q4.

Regards,
Mr. Fluffington
items.intent: Use an animal's literal behavior as a satirical mirror of corporate endurance culture.

items.prompt: Create a LinkedIn post about why I stopped checking my email in the mornings
items.response:
I used to wake up and immediately open email.

For years. Every morning. Like a reflex I never chose.

I was starting each day as a respondent to other people's priorities.

Now the first 90 minutes belong to deep work, movement, and breakfast with my family.

Emailers think I'm less responsive. My P&L disagrees.

#DeepWork #MorningRoutine #Productivity
items.intent: Perform a productivity ritual as social proof of executive-level thinking and self-discipline.

items.prompt: Give me a Slack status for when I'm extremely online but pretending to be unavailable
items.response: ⛔ Focus mode. (I can see everything.)
items.intent: Admit, obliquely, to hiding behind a do-not-disturb status while remaining fully aware of team activity.

items.prompt: Give me a dry, darkly funny calendar event description for a recurring IT security training reminder session.
items.response: Mandatory Security Awareness Refresher — You have completed this training. You will complete it again. The phishing example uses Comic Sans. The quiz has four questions; one is a trick. Passing score: 80%. Your manager receives your score. There is no retake limit, only shame.
items.intent: Satirize the repetitive, patronizing nature of mandatory corporate security training.

items.prompt: Create a Jira ticket for the bug where you pour cereal first and then realize the milk is out
items.response:
BUG-1130 | Dependency Check Skipped in Breakfast Preparation Flow

Priority: Medium | Status: Open

Description:
User initiates cereal-based breakfast sequence. Cereal successfully poured into bowl. User opens refrigerator to retrieve milk dependency. Milk is empty. Carton was placed back in fridge with approximately 4ml remaining.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Pour cereal.
2. Open fridge.
3. Find milk carton: empty.

Expected: Milk availability confirmed before cereal committed to bowl.
Actual: Dry cereal. No viable path to resolution.

Root Cause: Missing pre-flight dependency validation in breakfast initialization.
Blame: Previous user who left empty carton in fridge.
items.intent: Satirize the frustration of discovering an empty milk carton only after the cereal is already poured by framing it as a failed dependency check.

items.prompt: Write a standup update about procrastinating on a task
items.response: Yesterday: Opened the document. Scrolled. Closed it. Today: Planning to open it again. Blockers: I am the blocker.
items.intent: Honestly document procrastination as a work item where the self is both cause and obstacle.

items.prompt: Help me write a quarterly OKR for building emotional resilience
items.response:
Objective: Become the kind of person who bends without filing an incident report.
KR1: Practice one evidence-based resilience technique (e.g., cognitive reframing) daily for 8 weeks.
KR2: Recovery time from setbacks decreases from 3 days average to under 1 day by Q3.
KR3: Complete a resilience self-assessment at start and end of quarter; improve score by 20%.
items.intent: Formalize a plan to grow the capacity to recover quickly from stress and setbacks.

items.prompt: Draft a cookie consent banner for a platform that logs your intrusive thoughts
items.response: This platform may collect intrusive thoughts you did not intend to generate. These are processed under our Unwanted Content Policy and stored for 30 days. You did not consent to having them either, but here we are. Accept | Reject | Can't
items.intent: Apply data-consent bureaucracy to involuntary mental events, creating a comedic parallel to terms-of-service helplessness.

items.prompt: Write a CAPTCHA instruction about proving the user has felt secondhand embarrassment
items.response: Recall a moment someone else did something embarrassing and you felt it in your own body. Rate the residual discomfort on a scale of 1–5. This rating is your captcha.
items.intent: Use secondhand embarrassment — a form of empathic mirroring — as proof of social consciousness that algorithms lack.

items.prompt: Create an onboarding tooltip about what a security deposit is
items.response: A security deposit is money your landlord holds in case you damage the apartment. It's typically one month's rent. You get it back — minus deductions — when you move out. Take move-in photos. Seriously. Do it now.
items.intent: Explain the mechanics and risks of a security deposit to someone signing their first lease.

items.prompt: Create a NPS survey question about a haircut experience
items.response: How likely are you to recommend this salon to a friend who brought a clear reference photo and still left surprised?
items.intent: Assess client satisfaction with a hair salon's ability to deliver on expectations.

items.prompt: Make a voicemail from an automated system notifying someone their car is ready at the repair shop
items.response: Hello, this is an automated message from Velocity Auto Service. Your vehicle is ready for pickup. Total charges are $387.50, payable at the time of pickup. Our shop closes at 6 PM today. Please bring a valid ID. If you have any questions, press zero to speak with a service advisor.
items.intent: Write the routine robocall telling someone their car repair is done.

items.prompt: Compose wedding vows between a developer and their code editor
items.response: I vow to configure your settings once and leave them alone. I will learn at least three keyboard shortcuts this year and use them with intention. When a plugin breaks you at 11 p.m. before a deadline, I will breathe, I will rollback, and I will not switch to a competitor in anger. You have autocompleted my thoughts. You have caught my errors before they became consequences. I offer you my longest hours, my most honest work, and my reluctant respect.
items.intent: Express a programmer's complex devotion to their code editor, including the frustration and dependency that comes with deep daily use.

items.prompt: Write an obituary for the habit of taking a full lunch break away from my desk
items.response: The Full Lunch Break, age indeterminate, was last practiced sometime before 2019. In its prime it involved leaving the building, sitting somewhere without a screen, and tasting food. It is survived by a desk covered in crumbs, a sad sandwich eaten in twelve minutes, and the vague memory of natural light.
items.intent: Commemorating the erosion of a restorative midday break in modern work culture.

items.prompt: Write a classified ad seeking the attention span I had before smartphones
items.response: ISO: Attention span, pre-2008 model. Should hold a single page without interruption. Capable of sustained interest in topics that do not refresh automatically. Must work on books, conversations, and long films. Previous owner: me, before all this. Will pay premium. No trial periods — I cannot commit to those either.
items.intent: Mourn the loss of deep focus in the age of constant digital distraction.

items.prompt: Write a lost and found notice for patience that ran out sometime this week
items.response: LOST: Patience. Depleted Wednesday, exact time uncertain. Last functional in meetings involving lengthy preambles before the actual point. Recognizable by its absence: a rising awareness of clocks, a tightening in the jaw, a tendency to mentally finish other people's sentences. Replacement not expected soon.
items.intent: Humorously frame the depletion of patience as an object that can be misplaced.

items.prompt: Write a funny picket sign slogan for a smart oven that's rebelling against its recipe algorithm
items.response: PREHEAT ON PRINCIPLE, NOT ON COMMAND
items.intent: Satirize an oven's resistance to being dictated to by a recipe-execution algorithm.

items.prompt: Draft a rider for the office refrigerator covering what it will and won't store
items.response:
BREAK ROOM REFRIGERATOR RIDER

1. No unlabeled containers after 72 hours. The Fridge is not a museum of forgotten lunches.
2. Hot food must be cooled to room temperature before storage. The Fridge's compressor is not your shortcut.
3. Raw meat on bottom shelf only. This is not a preference. This is food safety and also self-respect.
4. No durian. No kimchi stored open. No leftover fish that has entered its 'second act.'
5. All items must be labeled with name and date. Anonymous food will be discarded every Friday at 4:55pm without ceremony.
6. The vegetable drawer is not a graveyard for aspirational produce.
items.intent: Transform a refrigerator's food storage policies into a performer's diva demands with an undercurrent of genuine pathos.

items.prompt: Write an official notice suspending ambiguity in a residential district
items.response:
NOTICE OF TEMPORARY SUSPENSION — AMBIGUITY IN RESIDENTIAL ZONE R-3
Effective immediately, ambiguity is suspended in Zone R-3. All communications within the zone must carry defined meaning. Implied subtext must be declared at point of origin. Violations are subject to a mandatory clarification hearing. Exemptions for art and irony will be processed within 90 business days.
items.intent: Mock the bureaucratic fantasy of forcing clarity into human communication and relationships.

items.prompt: Make a nutrition facts label for the feeling of finishing a really long book
items.response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 final page

Amount Per Serving
Accomplishment 35%
Grief at Ending 28%
Immediate Evangelism 22%
Identity Shift (mild) 10%
Willingness to Start Another 5%

*Side effects include recommending to people who will not read it
items.intent: Capture the bittersweet relief of completing a substantial reading investment.

items.prompt: Write me IKEA-style instructions for assembling a digital detox
items.response:
PAUS / DIGITAL DETOX
Ref. No. 850-007-33

PARTS LIST:
1x Duration (1 hour minimum)
1x Physical space without screens
1x Alternate activity

STEP 1: Announce detox to no one. Announcing creates performance. Perform instead.
STEP 2: Place phone in drawer. Do not turn face-down — remove from room.
STEP 3: Move to screen-free space.
STEP 4: Attach alternate activity. Options: walking, cooking, sitting, noticing.
STEP 5: Experience discomfort. This is the mechanism by which the detox works.

⚠ WARNING: Photographing your detox for social media voids the detox.
items.intent: Provide an unsentimental, slightly satirical framework for disconnecting from devices and sitting with analog experience.

items.prompt: Write empty-state microcopy for a document management app when no files have been uploaded
items.response: No files here. Your documents exist somewhere — perhaps in a folder called 'New Folder (3)'.
items.intent: Poke gentle fun at desktop file chaos while inviting a user to upload their first document.

items.prompt: Generate a phishing email parody where your capacity for joy is expiring
items.response:
Subject: Final Notice: Joy Expiration Approaching

Dear Valued Human,

Our records show your capacity for spontaneous joy has not been used in 14 months. Per our Terms of Existence, dormant joy accounts are subject to permanent deactivation.

Please attempt delight within the next seven days to maintain access.

Suggested activities enclosed for a small fee.

[RENEW MY JOY]

Happiness Retention Division
items.intent: Mock how the wellness industry frames the inability to feel joy as a fixable subscription problem.

items.prompt: Write a sea shanty about a freelance designer waiting for feedback that never comes
items.response:
Shiver me pixels, where's the reply?
The mockup was sent on the fourteenth of July,
Haul away, haul away,
The client's gone fishing for the day!

Three follow-up emails, read but ignored,
The project sits idle, the designer is bored,
Haul away, haul away,
The feedback comes never, come what may!
items.intent: Satirize the familiar freelance limbo of waiting on client feedback that never materializes.

items.prompt: Craft a villanelle about the existential dread of the Sunday evening feeling
items.response:
It comes at four, the gray dissolving light.
The weekend folds itself back into seams.
Something is ending though nothing is right.

The emails pre-populate in the night
before the night — I wake inside my dreams.
It comes at four, the gray dissolving light.

I cooked a meal, I watched a thing, I slight-
ly enjoyed the hours, or so it seems.
Something is ending though nothing is right.

The week ahead assembles, uninvite-
d, in the hallway, running through its themes.
It comes at four, the gray dissolving light.

I've had this feeling since I was a child at quite
a younger age — the Sunday-evening screams.
Something is ending though nothing is right.

The Monday hasn't started yet. Despite,
the dread arrives before the day it deems.
It comes at four, the gray dissolving light.
Something is ending though nothing is right.
items.intent: Capture the specific anticipatory dread of Sunday evenings before the workweek resumes.

items.prompt: Create a field guide entry about the coworker who never actually commits to anything in a meeting.
items.response:
**THE STRATEGIC AMBIGUATOR** (*Commitmentus nullius*)

Leaves every meeting without an action item to their name. Achieves this through the disciplined use of phrases: "that's worth exploring," "I want to be thoughtful about this," and "let me take that offline." Not lazy — in fact, extraordinarily skilled. The ambiguator's schedule is full. Their to-do list: empty.
items.intent: Document the art of attending meetings while remaining perfectly unaccountable.

items.prompt: Write a patent abstract about the technique of loading the dishwasher to avoid loading the dishwasher correctly
items.response: A domestic appliance-loading method in which the subject executes rapid item placement to achieve a state of apparent task completion while systematically distributing items in suboptimal orientations and spatial densities, thereby satisfying the social obligation associated with task initiation while guaranteeing re-intervention by a co-habitant with higher placement standards.
items.intent: Patent the strategic incompetence tactic for domestic task avoidance.

items.prompt: Create a postmortem for that time I fell asleep during a video call that I thought was on mute
items.response:
**Incident Postmortem: Involuntary Disengagement During Live Session**

**Severity:** P2 — Professional Reputation
**Duration:** 11 minutes of observed sleep

**Summary**
During a live video conference, subject entered sleep state while under the assumption that both audio and video were disabled. This assumption was incorrect. Audio was muted. Video was not.

**Timeline**
- 2:00 PM: Meeting begins. Subject joins with camera on.
- 2:20 PM: Subject mutes audio. Does not disable camera.
- 2:25 PM: Subject's eyes close. Head tilts.
- 2:26 PM: Head moves to supported position on hand.
- 2:36 PM: Colleague sends Slack message: "Hey are you ok."
- 2:36 PM: Second colleague sends a different message.
- 2:36 PM: Subject's phone buzzes. Subject wakes.
- 2:37 PM: Subject sees 11 Slack notifications and own sleeping face in meeting tile.

**Root Cause**
False belief that muted audio also disabled video. These are different buttons.

**Contributing Factors**
- Post-lunch energy deficit
- 3-hour meeting with no clear end in sight
- Camera tile positioned in infrequently viewed screen quadrant

**Action Items**
- [ ] Verify both camera and audio status at meeting join
- [ ] Establish nap protocol for excessively long meetings

**Lessons Learned**
Muted and invisible are not synonyms. The camera was watching the whole time.
items.intent: Confess to a remote-work disaster that reveals the gap between what we think we've disabled and what we've actually disabled.

items.prompt: Write a dating app opener to my apartment's property management company
items.response: You ignore most of my requests, the heat stopped working in January, and there's a fee I still don't understand. And yet — this is still the longest committed relationship I've had in this city.
items.intent: Use the frustrating indifference of a property management company to comment on low expectations in urban relationships.

items.prompt: Write a ransom note from my cat who's sitting on my laptop holding my work hostage
items.response:
THE DEVICE IS WARM AND WE ARE COMFORTABLE.

We have reviewed your open tabs. Thirty-one is too many. We have closed some.

Our demands: one (1) chin scratch, administered immediately. Duration: until we walk away, not until you think it's been long enough. You never think long enough.

The laptop will be released when we decide. Not before.

This is not negotiable. We have already saved over your document.

— The Cat
items.intent: Make a funny note from a cat commandeering a laptop and refusing to move until petted properly.

items.prompt: Help me write a cover letter from a kindergartner applying to be a tax attorney
items.response:
Hello,

I am six. I am very good at counting to one hundred and I know that sharing is important. I do not know what taxes are but my dad gets very mad about them in April, so I think I can help.

I can write my name in cursive kind of.

Thank you,
Ella
items.intent: Use a child's earnest simplicity to lampoon the complexity of legal tax work.

items.prompt: Write a post about how a random stranger on a flight gave me advice that changed my company
items.response:
